content: Gary launched VeeFriends in 2021 as a direct demonstration of his conviction that NFTs represented a genuine convergence of technology shift and business model shift simultaneously. Rather than simply talking about Web3, he built a project where NFT ownership functioned as access tokens to real-world events and experiences — making the intellectual property and community utility inseparable. He viewed it as the purest expression of what smart contracts could actually do for creators and brands.
implication: Builders entering Web3 should ask whether their project creates value that only exists because of the technology — not just slap a blockchain on an existing product. The convergence of IP, access, and ownership in a single token is the model worth studying.
source_ref: The Blockchain Is Changing What Humans Can Do on the Internet | 'Next With Novo' Podcast

content: Gary has consistently framed blockchain not primarily as a financial technology but as an infrastructure layer that fundamentally changes what humans can do on the internet — specifically around ownership and provenance. Before blockchain, digital files were infinitely copyable with no verifiable scarcity; after it, digital ownership becomes provable and programmable. He sees this as the equivalent of the shift from physical to digital commerce, except in the opposite direction: from infinite copies back to verifiable originals.
implication: Stop evaluating blockchain through a purely financial lens. The more durable question is: what behaviors and business models become possible when digital ownership is verifiable? That's where the 10-year opportunities live.
source_ref: The Blockchain Is Changing What Humans Can Do on the Internet | 'Next With Novo' Podcast

content: Gary has argued that most people dismissed NFTs the same way they dismissed social media, email, and the early web — by focusing on the speculative mania rather than the underlying capability. He acknowledged openly that there was significant froth in NFT prices in 2021-2022, but maintained that the noise of speculation should not be confused with the signal of the technology. The pattern of dismissal followed by regret is one Gary has personally witnessed repeat across every major platform shift of the past three decades.
implication: Train yourself to separate the speculative cycle from the structural shift. Every transformative technology arrives wrapped in a bubble. The bubble pops; the technology often stays. The investors and builders who stayed after the dot-com crash built the internet we live on.
source_ref: The Blockchain Is Changing What Humans Can Do on the Internet | 'Next With Novo' Podcast

content: Gary has repeatedly compared his bet on NFTs and Web3 to his early recognition of Facebook, Twitter, and YouTube as transformative attention platforms — bets most established operators dismissed as niche or irrelevant at the time. His filter in each case was the same: is real human behavior shifting here, or is this just a feature? When he saw communities forming around digital collectibles with genuine emotional attachment, he read that as the behavior signal that preceded platform maturity in every prior cycle.
implication: The leading indicator for a real platform shift is genuine emotional behavior from real humans — not press coverage, not venture capital volume. Find the community that would be devastated if the platform disappeared. That's where the attention is real.
source_ref: When You Hyper-Focus on the Results You Miss New Opportunities | The Quest Podcast

content: Gary's original thesis for VeeFriends was that intellectual property built on top of a blockchain community would compound in ways traditional IP could not — because the holders become stakeholders with aligned economic incentives to promote and protect the IP alongside the creator. He saw NFT ownership as converting passive fans into active co-owners of a brand, which is a fundamentally different relationship than a fan buying a t-shirt or a poster.
implication: Creators and brands should evaluate Web3 not as a new revenue channel but as a mechanism for converting audience into community ownership. The model shifts the marketing burden from the creator to an aligned network of stakeholders who benefit from the IP's success.
source_ref: The Blockchain Is Changing What Humans Can Do on the Internet | 'Next With Novo' Podcast

content: Gary has been explicit that he got into NFTs not primarily as a financial investment but because he believed the smart contract infrastructure would eventually change how every creative and commercial relationship on the internet is structured. He pointed to the ability to encode royalties into the transaction itself — so creators earn on every secondary sale without trusting any intermediary — as a genuinely new capability that had no analog in prior digital commerce.
implication: For creators, the royalty-in-the-contract mechanic is the most durable part of Web3 to understand. The ability to participate in the upside of secondary markets without relying on platform policy changes the long-term economics of creative work in a structural way.
source_ref: The Blockchain Is Changing What Humans Can Do on the Internet | 'Next With Novo' Podcast

content: Gary has consistently used the 1994 internet analogy to contextualize where blockchain and NFTs sat in their development cycle — arguing that people criticizing blockchain's clunky user experience or speculative excess in 2021 were equivalent to people dismissing the early web because dial-up was slow and most early websites were useless. He was personally shaped by being early on the internet in 1994 and has used that experience as a calibration tool for evaluating all subsequent platform shifts.
implication: Use the earliest internet period as your mental model for evaluating any new infrastructure technology. The correct question in 1994 wasn't 'is this website good?' — it was 'does this infrastructure eventually become unavoidable?' Ask the same question about Web3.
source_ref: '1994 Changed My Life': Gary Vaynerchuk on AOC and the internet

content: Gary has argued that the concept of digital scarcity — made possible by NFTs — is more culturally profound than most technologists appreciate, because scarcity has always been a primary driver of human desire and status signaling. Before blockchain, digital goods were infinitely reproducible and therefore culturally weightless as status objects. By making digital items genuinely scarce and verifiably owned, NFTs plugged digital collecting into the same psychological circuitry that has driven physical collecting for centuries.
implication: The psychology of collecting — scarcity, provenance, community, status — is ancient and durable. NFTs didn't invent that psychology; they applied existing infrastructure to it. Any project that taps these mechanics with genuine community identity will outlast the projects that are purely speculative.
source_ref: The Blockchain Is Changing What Humans Can Do on the Internet | 'Next With Novo' Podcast

content: Gary has openly acknowledged that many individual NFT projects will fail and that a lot of what was being sold during the 2021 peak had no underlying utility or community — meaning the speculation was detached from any real value creation. But he distinguished sharply between bad projects and a bad technology, drawing the parallel to the hundreds of failed dot-com companies that did not invalidate the internet itself. His confidence in the infrastructure remained intact even as he acknowledged most projects would go to zero.
implication: Maintain the discipline to separate asset-level due diligence from infrastructure-level conviction. Most specific bets in any new technology wave will fail. The game is identifying the technology wave correctly and then finding the few projects with genuine utility and community behind them.
source_ref: When You Hyper-Focus on the Results You Miss New Opportunities | The Quest Podcast

content: Gary has positioned AI-generated content as a force that will dramatically compress the cost of content production, effectively removing one of the last remaining barriers between a good idea and its execution. He has expressed the view that the strategic variable in a world of cheap AI-generated content shifts from production capability to creative direction and authentic perspective — because the tools will be equally available to everyone, so the differentiator becomes the human judgment guiding them.
implication: Invest less in production infrastructure and more in developing a distinctive creative point of view. When AI makes production essentially free, the scarce resource becomes originality, taste, and the authentic human signal that audiences can feel the difference between.
source_ref: Business Experts Reveal Growth Secrets! - with Gary Vee, Tom Bilyeu and more

content: Gary has consistently argued that his primary filter for any new technology is whether it changes human behavior at the consumer level, not whether it is technically sophisticated. He is notably less interested in the engineering architecture of blockchain than in the behavioral fact that people were assigning real emotional and financial value to digital objects — because that behavioral shift, once established, is what creates durable market opportunity regardless of which specific protocol wins.
implication: Evaluate emerging technologies through a behavioral lens first, technical lens second. Ask: are real humans already changing what they do because of this? If yes, the technical winners will emerge and the opportunity will be real. If no one's behavior has changed, the technology is still theoretical regardless of how impressive the engineering is.
source_ref: When You Hyper-Focus on the Results You Miss New Opportunities | The Quest Podcast

content: Gary has described the sports card and physical collectibles market as a direct predecessor and parallel to the NFT market — arguing that if you understand why a 1952 Mickey Mantle card holds value, you understand the fundamental mechanics behind why a verifiably scarce digital collectible can hold value. The underlying human drivers — nostalgia, scarcity, community, provenance, status — are identical. The difference is the delivery mechanism, not the psychology.
implication: Don't try to understand NFT markets in isolation. Study the 150-year history of physical collecting markets — trading cards, stamps, coins, art — to understand the durable mechanics. The technology is new; the human behavior it is plugging into is ancient.
source_ref: The Blockchain Is Changing What Humans Can Do on the Internet | 'Next With Novo' Podcast

content: Gary has made the case that the creator economy's biggest structural problem — platforms owning the relationship between creators and their audiences — gets solved by Web3 infrastructure. When a creator's audience relationship is encoded in tokens rather than stored in a platform's database, the platform can no longer revoke access, change the algorithm, or extract value from the creator-audience relationship without the creator's consent. He sees this as one of the most consequential shifts in the power balance between platforms and creators.
implication: Creators building on centralized platforms in 2024 are still tenants, not owners. The strategic question is how to progressively move the audience relationship to infrastructure you actually own — whether that is email, token-gated communities, or other direct connection mechanisms that no platform can switch off.
source_ref: The Blockchain Is Changing What Humans Can Do on the Internet | 'Next With Novo' Podcast

content: Gary has acknowledged making mistakes in timing and execution within his NFT bets — including being more vocal than he should have been during the peak of the market without sufficiently emphasizing the risk of specific projects. He has used his own experience with VeeFriends and the broader NFT market as a case study in the difference between being correct about a technology's long-term direction and being correct about the near-term price of assets in that technology. The two are separate questions that require separate answers.
implication: Public technology conviction comes with communication responsibility. If you are genuinely right about a 10-year technology shift, you still owe your audience an honest account of near-term volatility risk. Conflating infrastructure conviction with asset-price confidence is a failure of kind candor toward the people listening to you.
source_ref: When You Hyper-Focus on the Results You Miss New Opportunities | The Quest Podcast

content: Gary has argued that the metaverse — regardless of which company's specific vision wins — represents an inevitable expansion of the internet into spatial and immersive computing, and that brands and creators who dismiss it as science fiction are making the same mistake that brands made when they dismissed social media as a teenager's distraction. He doesn't position the metaverse as an immediate obligation for marketers, but as something that demands strategic monitoring and small-scale experimentation now rather than a wait-and-see posture.
implication: Run small metaverse experiments now at low cost, even if the ROI is not yet measurable. The cost of early experimentation is minimal; the cost of arriving late with no institutional knowledge is enormous. The brands that understood Facebook in 2007 didn't wait for proof — they ran experiments and built understanding while the platform was cheap.
source_ref: Business Experts Reveal Growth Secrets! - with Gary Vee, Tom Bilyeu and more

content: Gary's core thesis on AI has been consistent with his thesis on every prior technology wave: the question is not whether AI will change things but how fast and who will be positioned to benefit. He has specifically called out AI tools for content creation and marketing as representing the most significant shift in the attention economy since mobile, because they change the supply side of content production fundamentally — making it possible for a single operator to produce content at what was previously a team-scale volume.
implication: Small operators and solo creators gain the most from AI-powered content tools because they close the production gap with large teams the fastest. If you are a small creator or brand, AI tools are not optional — they are the leverage mechanism that makes the volume strategy viable without proportional headcount growth.
source_ref: Business Experts Reveal Growth Secrets! - with Gary Vee, Tom Bilyeu and more

content: Gary has described VeeFriends as a 40-year IP bet — not a 2-year NFT project — framing it explicitly in the same category as Disney or Pokémon: character-based IP that compounds in value across multiple generations and media formats. He chose to anchor the IP in blockchain not because it was trendy but because the ownership and royalty mechanics gave the IP a fundamentally different economic structure than traditional licensing, where the creator loses control of upside the moment IP is licensed downstream.
implication: If you are building character or world-based IP, evaluate blockchain-based ownership structures for what they do to your long-term economics — not just for the initial mint revenue. The structural change is in who captures value when the IP appreciates across secondary markets, licensing deals, and new media formats.
source_ref: The Blockchain Is Changing What Humans Can Do on the Internet | 'Next With Novo' Podcast

content: Gary has taken the position that the token-gated community model — where owning a specific NFT grants access to a community, event series, or set of services — is the most underappreciated business model innovation to come out of Web3, because it aligns the economic incentives of the community members with the value of the community itself. As the community becomes more valuable, the token appreciates, which attracts more committed members, which makes the community more valuable. He sees this flywheel as structurally superior to the ad-supported community model that dominates social platforms.
implication: Consider whether a token-gated community model fits your business before defaulting to a subscription or ad-supported model. The key question is whether your community's value compounds when members are economically aligned with its success — if yes, the token model creates a self-reinforcing growth dynamic that subscriptions cannot replicate.
source_ref: The Blockchain Is Changing What Humans Can Do on the Internet | 'Next With Novo' Podcast

content: Gary has consistently pushed back against the framing that Web3 and NFTs are primarily financial instruments, arguing that this framing is what attracts the wrong participants and sets up the inevitable disappointment cycle. His position is that the durable use cases for NFTs are in access, membership, provenance, and IP ownership — and that people who entered the market purely for price appreciation were never engaging with what the technology actually does well. The financial returns, in his view, are a byproduct of genuine utility creation, not the primary value proposition.
implication: Design NFT projects around what the token actually does for the holder beyond price appreciation. Utility-first design attracts holders who stay when prices drop; speculation-first design attracts flippers who leave. Community retention is the metric that predicts which projects survive a market correction.
source_ref: When You Hyper-Focus on the Results You Miss New Opportunities | The Quest Podcast

content: Gary has argued that the biggest mistake brands made with NFTs was approaching them as a marketing campaign with a defined start and end date rather than as infrastructure for a permanent community ownership model. Brands that treated their NFT drop as a campaign looked for ROI on the same timeline they would evaluate a paid media buy — and naturally concluded the experiment had failed. Brands that treated it as community infrastructure were asking a fundamentally different question with a fundamentally different time horizon.
implication: Before entering Web3, decide which question you are actually asking: campaign ROI or community infrastructure. The evaluation criteria, time horizon, and success metrics are completely different. Confusing the two is why most brand NFT projects produced disappointment on both dimensions.
source_ref: Business Experts Reveal Growth Secrets! - with Gary Vee, Tom Bilyeu and more

content: Gary has framed his personal investment in VeeFriends and Web3 as an act of intellectual consistency — having spent years telling others to bet on emerging platforms early, he felt obligated to make meaningful personal bets himself rather than merely advise others to take risks he was unwilling to take. He has described putting real capital into Web3 as a form of accountability to his own publicly stated philosophy about early platform adoption.
implication: The credibility of technology conviction is proportional to the skin you have in the game. Advising others to take early technology bets while staying personally safe is a form of intellectual dishonesty. If you genuinely believe in a technology shift, find the version of the bet that is appropriate to your means and make it.
source_ref: The Blockchain Is Changing What Humans Can Do on the Internet | 'Next With Novo' Podcast

content: Gary has pointed to the fact that in 2021, the majority of Fortune 500 companies had no Web3 strategy whatsoever — the same way they had no social media strategy in 2007. He sees this institutional lag as the predictable result of large organizations optimizing for near-term financial performance while new technology categories incubate at the edges. The opportunity for smaller operators and individual builders is always highest during the window when large players are still dismissing the category as irrelevant.
implication: Institutional indifference to a new technology is not evidence the technology is unimportant — it is frequently the opposite. The most durable competitive advantages in any technology shift go to the operators who build genuine capability during the years when the large players are still debating whether the category matters.
source_ref: When You Hyper-Focus on the Results You Miss New Opportunities | The Quest Podcast

content: Gary has observed that the emotional reaction people have to technologies like NFTs — dismissal, ridicule, moral outrage about environmental impact — mirrors almost exactly the reactions that greeted social media, smartphones, and the early internet. He is not dismissive of those concerns but uses the emotional intensity of the reaction itself as a signal that the technology is touching something real about how people relate to value, ownership, and identity. Strong emotional reactions, in his framework, are confirmation that the stakes are real.
implication: Pay attention to the emotional temperature of the criticism a new technology receives. Indifference means the technology is genuinely irrelevant; intense ridicule or moral panic often means it is disrupting something people care deeply about. The reaction is itself a signal about what is at stake.
source_ref: The Blockchain Is Changing What Humans Can Do on the Internet | 'Next With Novo' Podcast
